title: Laying porcelain floor tiles
subtitle: Prepare the floor, set out the grid, and bed large-format tiles
excerpt: How to lay porcelain floor tiles: checking the floor for level, finding the set-out point, mixing and spreading flexible tile adhesive, laying tiles to a grid with spacers, grouting, and sealing the perimeter joints.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Wall and Floor Tiling syllabus.

glossaryTerms.definition: The starting position for a tiling grid, chosen to centre the tile pattern in the room and minimise narrow cut tiles at the visible edges. Found by snapping chalk lines across the room at the mid-points of opposite walls.

Tile adhesive with a polymer additive that accommodates minor floor movement. Required for porcelain tiles (which are denser and less forgiving than ceramic) and on timber subfloors that flex slightly underfoot.

Applying a thin coat of adhesive directly to the back of a tile in addition to the adhesive on the floor. Required on large-format tiles (600 mm and above) to ensure full contact between the tile back and the adhesive bed and prevent hollow spots.

content.text: Porcelain floor tiles are dense, hard, and unforgiving of a poorly prepared substrate. A flexible adhesive bed, a true floor, and a well-planned grid are the three elements that determine whether the finished floor holds for decades or lifts within a few years. The job spans two days: adhesive curing time before grouting, and a further overnight cure before foot traffic.
Step 1, check and prepare the floor

The floor must be level within 3 mm over a 2-metre span. Check with a long spirit level or a straightedge. Fill dips and grind down high spots: a self-levelling floor compound covers dips up to about 50 mm in one pour; a grinding disc on an angle grinder knocks back high spots on a concrete floor. On a timber floor, all boards must be fixed firm (no movement underfoot), and the surface should be covered with 6 mm plywood screwed at 200 mm centres to provide a rigid, stable base.
Find the mid-point of two opposite walls and snap a chalk line across the floor. Repeat for the other two walls: the intersection is the

. Dry-lay a row of tiles from this point to one wall, including spacers, to check the cut at the wall. If the cut would leave a tile less than half a tile wide, shift the set-out point by half a tile. Repeat for the other axis.
Step 3, spread adhesive and bed tiles

to a smooth, stiff-peak consistency. Spread over an area of about 0.5 square metres with a notched trowel (6 mm notches for tiles up to 450 mm; 10 mm notches for tiles 600 mm and larger), combing the adhesive in parallel ridges. For tiles 600 mm and larger, use

: spread a thin even coat on the tile back with the flat of the trowel. Press each tile firmly into position, twisting slightly to collapse the adhesive ridges. Use a rubber mallet and a tile-beating block to bed each tile flat. Check level with the spirit level across every three or four tiles as you lay.
Step 4, allow to cure, then grout

Allow the adhesive to cure for at least 24 hours (48 hours in cool or damp conditions) before grouting. Apply floor grout with a rubber grout float, pressing into the joints diagonally. Clean the tile face with a damp sponge before the grout skins fully. Allow the grout to cure overnight before walking on the floor. Seal the perimeter joint between the tiled floor and the skirting board with flexible silicone sealant rather than grout, to allow for slight thermal movement in the floor.

attrs.symptom: Hollow sound when tapping a tile
attrs.cause: Air pocket beneath the tile from inadequate back-buttering or adhesive ridges not collapsing fully.
attrs.fix: If the tile has not cracked, it may hold. If it moves or cracks, relay with full back-butter and ensure the adhesive ridges collapse flat under the tile.

attrs.symptom: Tiles cracking after a few months
attrs.cause: Floor movement that the tile or adhesive cannot accommodate. Common on timber subfloors that were not adequately stiffened.
attrs.fix: Strip the failed tiles. Stiffen the subfloor with additional fixings or thicker plywood. Relay with flexible adhesive and back-butter.

attrs.cause: Tiles not fully bedded to the same level, or the floor has a high spot that was not ground back.
attrs.fix: Address the subfloor issue. On large-format tiles, use tile levelling clips and wedges during laying to hold adjacent tiles level until the adhesive sets.
